speaker: interviewee
text: Want me to continue? Or you will ask question?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. You were saying-- saying that we lost so many people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, mm-hmm. I think it's the responsibility of the next generation to stamp out anti-Semitism and intolerance in its tracks. I think it's their obligation to our [? martyred ?] relatives to condemn all kinds of manifestation of hatred against Jews and other people as well. This why we have the Anti-Defamation League to help us with this battle against intolerance among people. I feel strongly that mankind has learned its lesson. But as recent events show me, there is a tremendous amount of hatred among people, like in Bosnia or other people, between Muslims and Croats and Serbs. And this should not happen. There's really no reason why one race should try to eradicate another race or one religion try to eradicate or impose itself over another religion. We are all people of God. And we should act accordingly, and we should respect each other's religion, just like my mother's uncle and aunt did it with each other. And this is the way I would like to have my grandchildren and the future generation to see from this standpoint. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I have to mention another little episode in my life, which was in 1988. This was the time when my daughter-- whom I dearly love, and she's a very hardworking, very intelligent person with her PhD degree in-- in toxicology and environmental health science-- she was pregnant with her first child. And I could not attend the birth or the bris of her firstborn, who was Andrew. I was recuperating from an open heart surgery, a triple bypass surgery. And I have to tell you, if it were not for my wife, I-- I would not be here today, because Mary watched those nurses carefully. She-- she kept the-- the doctors on close watch. She monitored me all the time. She was at my side. She forced me to do breathing exercises because my lungs collapsed. She forced the spoon into me when I-- even when I didn't want to. And this why I'm saying that I have two women who were my guardian angels. [CRYING] Excuse me. One was my mother and who, through the bad years, kept pushing me to-- to walk and march and to do the work-- the hard work. And that's how I survive. And the other one is my wife, who was standing behind me and besides me all these 42 years, through good and bad times, which were, of course, many. And beginnings are not pleasant, and beginnings are hard. And when you don't speak the language, to start a new life is very, very difficult. I remember, even in the United States, I found racial or religious discrimination. I was applying to a job, which would have been a large job, what they call the cashier's job, an officer job, in a large brokerage firm. I cannot name the name, but it's one of the largest brokerage firms in this-- insurance brokerage firms in this country. And I passed the first man who was interviewing me. And he said, everything looks fine with your application. I want you to see the next person, who was some vice president or executive vice president. And he started very amicably, very cordially. And he started asking me what kind of a country club do I belong to. And I turned over to him and I said, look, I do not belong to a country club, because country clubs generally discriminate against Jews. And I don't play golf, and I don't-- and I don't belong to any country club, and I don't wish to. And I said, and nevertheless, I'm Jewish, and my wife is a converted Jew. She was not born as a Jew. And he turned me, he thanked me for the interview. And he said, you will hear from me. I never heard from him. The only thing what I heard from the agency was that they didn't want me. And I think it was simply because he found out I was Jewish. And this was here in the United States of America. So what I wanted to say is there is discrimination. There is the racial hatred, probably not as much nowadays in the-- in the-- in the USA, but in those years. And another example-- what I can say is when I applied for a particular position I had to do a written test. And in the written test, they asked me about American history. And I was pleading with the head of the employment department or personnel department. I said, don't give me this test. Give me a mathematical test. I will be doing fine. I will be doing with flying colors. I do not know American history. I was not born here. I just came out, and I don't know the American history. She wouldn't let me. And of course, I failed the test. I never got the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this why I'm saying-- she was-- Mary was standing besides me. She was teaching my daughter the-- the good from the bad, to distinguish between good and bad, which she did all her life. And-- and my daughter is very successful raising her two kids, raising them as Jewish little boys. Because her husband's not Jewish, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And John is a-- is a very fine person. He has a doctorate in chemical engineering. He takes the boys to the temple. He puts on his yarmulke. He sings with them the Hanukkah songs. He prepares for Hanukkah. He is probably a better Jew than-- than I am. My daughter is involved in a temple as a vice president. And besides raising her two boys, she is-- she is teaching at the University of Texas, at the graduate school level, toxicology courses, because that was-- that is one of her PhD degrees. And I'm very proud of both of my kids, Judy and John. And, of course, the grandchildren were tremendous kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what has been your religious affiliation since you came to this country? What-- were you Conservative? Reformed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, once we belonged to the Conservative temple. But basically, I think we are Reformed, just because of the fact that it's easier to read the Reformed prayer books in English for my wife to understand. And my daughter also belongs to a Reformed temple because John. And John can better follow it since he-- he doesn't read the Hebrew text. But I read the Hebrew text, and I can understand it. I know the songs. And-- but I'm not a devout person. We are going to-- to the temple on High Holy Days, and we observe the holidays. And, of course, we observe the [HEBREW] services. And that's about it, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had asked you, during the preliminary interview, I actually said to you that when we end, I was going to ask you to leave a message to your wife, to your children, your grandchildren, and the rest of the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was thinking about it, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it is extremely difficult to understand how the extermination of 6 million Jews could have happened. You have to put yourself in this particular scenario, when the Nazi propaganda took place in all those countries. And all these people were inflamed with this Nazi rhetoric and Nazi propaganda, which inflamed them. They used all kinds of things against Jews-- that Jews killed Christian boys or this and that. And this turned people against Jews. I think we have to make a concerted effort to try to stop all kinds of manifestations of hatred against Jews. And we have to stop intolerance among people. It will be up to the future generation, like Andrew and David, to-- to do this fight and never let go of this fight against racial intolerance and religious hatred. This will be the only fitting memorial to my father and my uncle and my grandmother who perished in-- in the Holocaust with only God's name on their lips. And-- and then we know that their memory will not fade from the people's mind. Because my grandchildren and the children of my grandchildren will do their utmost to-- to remember them by doing work for the eradication of racial and religious hatred. And basically, this is my-- my message to the future generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your testimony today will-- will help educate many generations to learn about the Holocaust and to hopefully end hatred. And I'd like to thank you very much for opening up your home and your heart to us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please introduce a lovely lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This lovely lady is my wife of 42 years, Mary. And she has been with me for good and bad for the last 42 years. So here she is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mary--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello. Your husband's a big fan of yours, obviously. And I've spent a couple of hours with you before today, and I could see why.

speaker: interviewee
text: But [INAUDIBLE] too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aw. [LAUGHS] What I want to know-- you converted to Judaism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you realized what had happened in Europe to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually after the war, after I can say 1945. I was a very silly child. I was very, you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: Playful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Playful. And somehow I didn't know what's happening around me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you had lots of Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- well, after the war, not before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents never talked about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the youngest of three girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think I was a little bit spoiled. And being a very playful kid, I wasn't serious enough to talk anything about it because my mind was always just on playing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came home and told your parents that you were going to marry Ivan, what was their reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first I got home and told them that I met a very nice guy, and I would like them to meet him. And they start-- started talking about Ivan. And I told them that he's Jewish. That wasn't any problem with my parents, and I think because of the fact they weren't the same religion either. My mother was Roman Catholic and a fairly religious person. And my father was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Calvinist, Calvin--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Calvinist.

speaker: interviewee
text: Calvinist church.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not religious whatsoever, as far as I know. So the being Jewish, it wasn't any big problem in the family, as long as we like each other, and that was the main issue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you decided to convert--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did your parents take that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents, as I said, didn't care one way or another. Why I decided to-- I am basically a very religious person. Why I decided to convert, by that time I already knew what happened around me when I was a-- a silly kid, and what happened with the Jews because I had a lot of Jewish friends. And I heard my boss where I was working. I was work-- working in a university, secretary of a-- of the dean. He was Jewish also-- also. And I've heard, and I educated myself what happened. And I decided if we want to have a family, we have to have the same religion. I didn't want to have just like my parents, where my mother and the three girls went to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Roman. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: --Roman Catholic Church, and my father was home. Because he wasn't Catholic, and he wasn't religious either. So I wanted to have a family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Life.

speaker: interviewee
text: --religious life. And I could not ask my husband to convert to my religion when he and his whole family went through such terrible things, just because of the fact that they were Jews. So I offered. Naturally, I'm going to convert. And yeah, I'm going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: She did it in a mikveh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --raise our-- yeah, I was nine months pregnant. [LAUGHS] And that's how [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: She immersed.

speaker: interviewer
text: She immersed herself in holy water. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Holy water.

speaker: interviewee
text: To me-- to me, the person counts, not the religion. You can be religious in your religion or the other one in their religion. But there is one God, and that's the main point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: In closing, is there anything else either one of you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: To--

speaker: interviewer
text: To each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the future generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: A message the way I asked your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you since 42 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want to love you another 42 years. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's perfect.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This picture is of my maternal grandmother, and her name is Renner, R-E-N-N-E-R. And the picture was taken in 1927 in Hungary in Komarom, in the city of Komarom. My grandmother tried to cut her veins on the way to Auschwitz but was taken off the-- the transport, and they stopped her bleeding. And eventually, they return back her-- her to the transport, and she was perished in Auschwitz. She was selected to die. This picture represents my father and my mother-- my father, Dr. Solti, Elemer, and my mother, Renner, Maria, nickname [? Mitzi. ?] The picture was taken approximately in 1930 in Szolnok, Hungary. This picture represent my maternal uncle. His name is Renner, Imre. The picture is from 1925 in the city of Komarom, Hungary. He perished in the Holocaust. This picture represents my mother and myself. The year was approximately 1940. The picture was taken in the city of Szolnok, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Solti, Maria, maiden name Renner. Her nickname is [? Mitzi ?]. My name is Ivan Solti. In Hungarian, I was called Solti Ivan [? Andras. ?] This picture represents my father and myself. My father's name is Dr. Solti, Elemer. And my name is Solti Ivan [? Andras ?] or Ivan Solti. The picture was taken approximately in 1937 in Budapest-- in Hungary. This picture represents my daughter and my son-in-law and her two children. My son-in-law's name is John Wojciak, W-O-J-C-I-A-K. And my daughter's name is Judith Wojciak, born Solti, S-O-L-T-I. My two grandchildren are Andrew Alan Wojciak and David Michael Wojciak. The picture was taken in 1993 in Houston, Texas.
